docs.text:
All held items are fully implemented in battle via HeldItemEffects.ts.

Item Effect Tier Min Floor Wide Lens ×1.1 move accuracy 1 1 Shell Bell Heal 1/8 of damage dealt 1 2 Kings Rock +10% flinch chance on all damaging moves 1 2 Expert Belt ×1.2 damage on super effective hits 2 2 Zoom Lens ×1.2 accuracy when moving second 2 3 Eviolite ×1.5 Def and SpDef if not fully evolved 2 3 Leftovers Restore 1/16 max HP per turn 2 3 Black Sludge Poison types: +1/16 HP/turn; others: −1/8 HP/turn 2 3 Focus Sash Survive one KO hit at full HP; consumed on trigger 3 4 Rocky Helmet Deal 1/6 HP to contact attackers 3 4 Assault Vest Special damage ÷1.5; blocks status moves 3 4 Choice Band ×1.5 Attack (physical); locks first move used 3 4 Choice Specs ×1.5 Sp. Attack (special); locks first move used 3 4 Choice Scarf ×1.5 Speed; locks first move used 3 4 Life Orb ×1.3 damage; 10% max HP recoil per hit 3 4

docs.title: Choice Item Locking
docs.text: When a Pokémon holds a Choice item and uses a move, lockedMoveIndex is set on BattlePokemon. All other moves are greyed out in the move menu. The lock clears on switch-out. The engine blocks non-locked moves with a "can't use that move!" message.

docs.title: Zoom Lens Condition
docs.text: Zoom Lens only applies its accuracy bonus when the bearer moves after the opponent. The movingSecond flag is derived from turn order in resolveTurnToEvents and threaded into resolveMove → accuracyCheck.

docs.title: Loot Generation
docs.text:
generateLootChoices(floor, rng, count, luck, party) builds the item pool:

  1. Filter by item.minFloor <= floor
  2. Filter conditionals — full-heal is excluded if no party member has a status condition
  3. Roll tier for each candidate (base tier + upgrade chance cascade)
  4. Weight candidates by tier, return count choices

The party parameter (array of RunPokemon) is passed from both ItemScene and RewardHandler so conditional filtering is always team-aware.

docs.title: Bag UI
docs.text: The BagViewer component (src/ui/components/BagViewer.ts) provides a scrollable overlay: - One item per row (56px height) - Sorted by tier descending (rarest first) - Left accent bar colour-coded by tier - Shows name, description, count, and HELD/USE badge - Finger drag scrolling with geometry mask clipping - Mouse wheel support - Tapping a row opens PartyPicker in equip-item or use-item mode as appropriate

docs.title: Removed Items
docs.text:
The following items were removed from the loot pool as they are made redundant by Full Heal:

  • ~~Antidote~~ (cured poison)
  • ~~Awakening~~ (cured sleep)
  • ~~Burn Heal~~ (cured burn)

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Solwara uses a Slay the Spire-inspired procedural node map. Each zone (grief stage) has its own seeded map generated at run start. The map is the same every time you enter a zone with the same seed, so reloading mid-run returns you to exactly where you were.

docs.title: Grid Structure
docs.text:
7 columns × 15 floors + 1 gym node (floor 14)

  • Floors are 0-indexed (0–14)
  • Floor 14 is always the gym — a single convergence node
  • Floors 0–13 are content floors
Floor 14:  [           GYM           ]
Floor 13:  [rest] [SETTLEMENT] [rest] [rest] [rest]
  ...
Floor  1:  [─] [─] [─] [─] [─] [─]   (6 branching paths)
Floor  0:  [         WILD            ]

docs.title: Path Generation (StS-style)
docs.text:
1. Floor 0 is always a single wild node centred on the map — all paths originate from it
  2. 6 independent seeded paths walk upward from floor 1 to floor 13
  3. Each path steps to one of the 3 nearest columns on the next floor
  4. No-crossing invariant — paths maintain left-to-right ordering (path N never exceeds path N+1's column)
  5. Any node on floors 1–13 unreachable by any path is pruned
  6. All surviving floor-13 nodes connect to the single gym node

docs.title: Floor Guarantees
docs.text: Floor Rule 0 Single wild node, centred horizontally 1–4 elite_trainer not eligible 5+ elite_trainer allowed 8 One guaranteed item node at a seeded column; rest random 7, 9, 10, 11, 12 Rival floor — one picked per zone (seeded). All nodes on that floor become rival 13 One settlement node at a seeded column; all others are rest. All connect to gym 14 Single gym node

docs.title: Quality Rules
docs.text:
• Elite/rest separation — elite_trainer and rest cannot be adjacent on any path
  • Branching variety — a node with 2+ outgoing connections cannot send both to nodes of the same type

docs.title: Rival Placement
docs.text: The rival floor is chosen from [7, 9, 10, 11, 12] (0-indexed) at run start using the zone seed. Floor 8 (guaranteed item) is excluded from rival eligibility. Because the entire rival floor is locked to type rival, any path the player takes will encounter the rival exactly once per zone.

docs.title: Visual Layout
docs.text: Isometric stagger — odd-numbered floors are offset horizontally by COL_W / 2 (60px), creating a triangular grid. Connection lines are drawn straight (no midpoint kink), letting the stagger provide natural visual separation.

docs.title: Layout Constants
docs.text:
Constant Value Notes COLS 7 Columns per floor COL_W 120px Horizontal spacing LAYER_H 72px Vertical spacing between floors MAP_PAD_TOP 48px Padding above floor 0 CANVAS_W 1280px

Floor 0 and floor 14 (single nodes) are always rendered at CANVAS_W / 2 = 640px.

docs.title: Camera / Navigation
docs.text:
• Map starts centred on the first available node at zoom 1.6×
  • Pan — drag anywhere (pointer down + move)
  • Pinch zoom — two-finger pinch (activePointers: 2 in Phaser game config)
  • Scroll wheel — zoom in/out on desktop
  • Min zoom 0.3×, max 2.0×

docs.title: Node Selection
docs.text:
• Available nodes show a pulsing glow ring
  • Tap a node to enter it
  • Once a node is entered, it is locked as in-progress (currentNodeId in save)
  • Node is only marked visited on return — reloading mid-node sends the player back to it
  • Only one node per floor layer can be visited per zone run

docs.title: Implementation Files
docs.text: File Responsibility src/systems/map/MapGenerator.ts Pure data — generates the node graph src/scenes/map/NodeMapScene.ts Thin orchestrator — init, routing, state src/scenes/map/NodeMapRenderer.ts All drawing — nodes, lines, grid, legend src/scenes/map/NodeMapInput.ts Pan, zoom, pinch src/scenes/map/NodeMapGlobals.ts Shared module state

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Solwara uses a meta progression system. Each run starts fresh but permanent unlocks accumulate over time.

docs.title: What Persists Between Runs
docs.text:
• Unlocked starters per role
  • Move Mastery unlocks per role
  • Charm pool expansions
  • Legendary Charm (if purchased)
  • Pokédex Charm per role (if unlocked)
  • Story discoveries — things learned about the world stay learned
  • The Hope Badge slot — always visible in the badge case, always a reminder
  • Solwara Coins balance

docs.title: What Resets Each Run
docs.text:
• Your team
  • Items
  • In-run money (minus 10% converted to Solwara Coins on loss)
  • Current charms equipped
  • Node map progress

docs.title: Solwara Coins
docs.text:
• Persistent currency, never lost
  • On a failed run: 10% of in-run money converts to Solwara Coins
  • Encourages pushing further even in failing runs — more money earned = more coins
  • Spent at the meta shop between runs

docs.title: Meta Shop
docs.text: Accessible between runs. Purchased with Solwara Coins.

docs.title: Move Mastery
docs.text: Role-exclusive moves teachable to starters. Moves the Pokémon could not normally learn in mainline. Thematically tied to each roles personality — see move_mastery.md.

docs.title: Random Charm Unlock
docs.text: Spend coins to permanently add a new charm to the in-run pool. Random — you do not choose which charm is added.

docs.title: Legendary Charm
docs.text: One time purchase. Adds legendaries to the rescue node pool. Protected legendaries never available: Kyogre, Groudon, Dialga, Arceus.

docs.title: Pokédex Charm (per role)
docs.text: Unlocked by beating a run with that role — not purchased with coins. Allows selecting any previously caught Pokémon in base form as your starter for that role. Role narrative still applies. Move Mastery still applies to chosen starter. One charm per role — Hero, Doctor, Accepted, Defector (when available).

docs.title: Starter Unlock System
docs.text:
• Each role has a pool of 5 starters
  • Default starter available immediately
  • Beating a run unlocks a random starter from that roles pool
  • Legendary starters have 10% unlock chance
  • If only the legendary starter remains locked, next unlock is guaranteed
  • Beating a role 4 times guarantees the legendary unlock path

docs.title: Charms (Relics)
docs.text: Found during runs at loot nodes, shops, and events. Passive effects that modify the run. Each run builds a different charm loadout. New charms added to the pool permanently via meta shop purchases.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Move Mastery is the system by which starters learn moves they could not normally learn in mainline Pokémon. These moves are role-exclusive, thematically tied to each roles personality, and purchased from the meta shop using Solwara Coins.

Move Mastery applies to whichever Pokémon is your starter — including Pokédex Charm selections.

docs.title: The Hero
docs.text:
Brave, selfless, protective. Carries the weight of others expectations.

Move Thematic Reason Sacred Sword A heros blade — righteous, precise Wish Sacrifices your turn for someone else Aura Sphere Righteous energy, never misses Reversal Stronger when weakened — never gives up Close Combat Throws yourself in regardless of consequence

docs.title: The Doctor
docs.text:
Curious, driven, interventionist. Always moving, always solving.

Move Thematic Reason Trick Room Completely subverts the expected order Future Sight Sees what is coming before it arrives Psych Up Adapts to whatever it faces Skill Swap Always has a plan involving switching things around Miracle Eye Sees through barriers, finds what others miss

docs.title: The Accepted
docs.text:
Calm, unbothered, at peace — but perhaps for the wrong reasons.

Move Thematic Reason Recover Simply continues Amnesia Lets go of something Rest Complete surrender to the moment Purify Cleanses what it touches Ingrain Plants itself and accepts where it is

docs.title: The Defector
docs.text: To be designed after launch roles are fully written.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Solwara uses a custom move system with all 789 learnset moves declared as typed class instances with attribute composition. Mechanics follow Gen IX rules. The legacy MoveEffects.ts dispatch table has been deleted — all moves route through MoveRegistry.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
src/systems/battle/moves/
  core/
    Move.ts          — base classes: AttackMove, StatusMove, SelfStatusMove
    MoveAttr.ts      — abstract attribute base
    MoveContext.ts   — context passed to every attr.apply()
    MoveId.ts        — const object mapping 789 move names to PokéAPI numeric IDs
    MoveRegistry.ts  — Map<MoveId, Move>, populated at boot via registerAll()
  attrs/
    StatusEffectAttr.ts      — status condition at chance %
    StatChangeAttr.ts        — stat raise/lower on self or target (incl. accuracy/evasion)
    RecoilAttr.ts            — recoil as % of damage dealt
    DrainAttr.ts             — heal attacker as % of damage dealt
    HealAttr.ts              — heal self as % of max HP
    FlinchAttr.ts            — flinch at chance %
    ConfuseAttr.ts           — confusion at chance %
    FixedDamageAttr.ts       — exact fixed damage (Dragon Rage = 40)
    LevelDamageAttr.ts       — damage = user's level (Night Shade, Seismic Toss)
    MultiHitAttr.ts          — 2-5 hits with Gen 5+ weight distribution
    HpRatioDamageAttr.ts     — power scales with user HP (Flail, Water Spout)
    WeightDamageAttr.ts      — power scales with weight (Low Kick, Heavy Slam)
    SpeedRatioDamageAttr.ts  — power scales with speed (Gyro Ball, Electro Ball)
    TargetHpDamageAttr.ts    — power scales with target HP (Wring Out, Crush Grip)
    StageScaledDamageAttr.ts — power scales with positive stat stages (Stored Power)
    TwoTurnMoveAttr.ts       — charge turn + release (Fly, Dig, Solar Beam, etc.)
  moves/
    NormalMoves.ts / NormalMoves2.ts
    FireMoves.ts / ElectricMoves.ts / WaterMoves.ts / GrassMoves.ts
    IceMoves.ts / FightingMoves.ts / PoisonMoves.ts / GroundMoves.ts
    FlyingMoves.ts / PsychicMoves.ts / BugMoves.ts / RockMoves.ts
    GhostMoves.ts / DragonMoves.ts / DarkMoves.ts / SteelMoves.ts / FairyMoves.ts

docs.title: Declaring a Move
docs.text:
// Damaging move with burn chance
new AttackMove(MoveId.FLAMETHROWER, 'fire', 'special', 90, 100, 15, 10)
  .attr(StatusEffectAttr, 'burn', 10)
  .implemented('full');

// Variable power (Gyro Ball)
new AttackMove(MoveId.GYRO_BALL, 'steel', 'physical', null, 100, 5, -1)
  .attr(SpeedRatioDamageAttr, 'gyro')
  .implemented('full');

// Two-turn move (Fly)
new AttackMove(MoveId.FLY, 'flying', 'physical', 90, 95, 15, -1)
  .attr(TwoTurnMoveAttr, '{name} flew up high!', true)  // true = invisible
  .implemented('full');

// Stat boost
new SelfStatusMove(MoveId.DRAGON_DANCE, 'dragon', 20)
  .attr(StatChangeAttr, 'atk', 1, true)
  .attr(StatChangeAttr, 'spe', 1, true)
  .implemented('full');

docs.title: AttackMove Constructor
docs.text:
new AttackMove(id, type, category, power, accuracy, pp, chance, priority?, target?)
Param Type Notes id MoveId PokéAPI numeric ID type Type e.g. 'fire', 'electric' category 'physical' \| 'special' power number \| null null for variable-power or status moves accuracy number \| null null = never misses pp number Max PP chance number Secondary effect chance; pass -1 if unused priority number Default 0 target MoveTarget Default 'opponent'

docs.title: Implementation Status
docs.text:
Every move is tagged with .implemented():

Status Meaning 'full' Works correctly including all secondary effects 'partial' Damage applies but a mechanic is missing (switch-out, HP-lock, etc.) 'not' Declaration exists; engine shows "(not implemented)" message

docs.title: Engine Integration
docs.text:
BattleEngine.resolveMove() checks MoveRegistry.getMove(slot.moveId) first:

  1. Two-turn charge — if TwoTurnMoveAttr present and not already charged, sets attacker.chargingMove, emits charge message, returns. On the next turn the charge is detected and skipped to full resolution
  2. Priority — read from move.priority
  3. Variable power — checks FixedDamageAttr, LevelDamageAttr, HpRatioDamageAttr, WeightDamageAttr, SpeedRatioDamageAttr, TargetHpDamageAttr, StageScaledDamageAttr before calling calcDamage
  4. Multi-hit — MultiHitAttr rolls hit count, loops damage N times
  5. Effects — move.applyAttrs(ctx) runs all attrs with damage result
  6. Status events — status_change emitted so HUD refreshes immediately

If no registered move is found (should not happen with 789/789 coverage), shows "(not implemented)" and returns.

docs.title: Variable Power Attrs
docs.text: Attr Mode Formula HpRatioDamageAttr('low') Flail, Reversal Gen IX table: 20→200 as HP% drops HpRatioDamageAttr('high') Water Spout, Eruption, Dragon Energy floor(150 × hp/maxHp), min 1 WeightDamageAttr('target') Low Kick, Grass Knot Target kg → 20/40/60/80/100/120 WeightDamageAttr('ratio') Heavy Slam, Heat Crash User/target ratio → 40/60/80/100/120 SpeedRatioDamageAttr('gyro') Gyro Ball floor(25 × target_spe / user_spe), max 150 SpeedRatioDamageAttr('electro') Electro Ball Speed ratio buckets → 40/60/80/120/150 TargetHpDamageAttr Wring Out, Crush Grip floor(120 × target_hp/maxHp), min 1 StageScaledDamageAttr Stored Power, Power Trip base + base × positive_stages

docs.title: Two-Turn Moves
docs.text: TwoTurnMoveAttr(chargeMessage, invisible?): - Charge turn: sets attacker.chargingMove = { moveIndex, invisible }, emits chargeMessage, consumes PP, returns early (no damage) - Release turn: engine detects attacker.chargingMove.moveIndex === moveIndex, clears it, falls through to full damage resolution - Invisible flag: true for Fly, Dig, Dive, Bounce, Phantom Force, Shadow Force — sets invisible: true on chargingMove. Note: accuracy bypass for invisible targets is not yet implemented in the engine - AI: chooseMove is bypassed when opponentActive.chargingMove is set; AI always uses the charged move index - Cleared: on faint (handleFaint) and on switch (resolveTurnToEvents switch block)

docs.title: Two-Turn Move List
docs.text: Move Charge message Invisible Fly "flew up high!" ✅ Dig "burrowed underground!" ✅ Dive "hid underwater!" ✅ Bounce "sprang up!" ✅ Phantom Force "vanished instantly!" ✅ Shadow Force "vanished instantly!" ✅ Sky Attack "is glowing!" ❌ Sky Drop "took the target into the sky!" ✅ Solar Beam "absorbed light!" ❌ Solar Blade "absorbed light!" ❌ Electro Shot "absorbed electricity!" ❌ Freeze Shock "became cloaked in a freezing light!" ❌ Ice Burn "became cloaked in a freezing light!" ❌ Skull Bash "tucked in its head!" ❌ Razor Wind "whipped up a whirlwind!" ❌ Geomancy "is absorbing power!" ❌

docs.title: What Is Not Yet Implemented
docs.text:
• Weather/terrain — Rain Dance, Sunny Day, Electric Terrain etc. (not)
  • Entry hazards — Stealth Rock, Spikes, Toxic Spikes, Sticky Web (not)
  • Protect variants — Protect, Detect, Baneful Bunker etc. (not)
  • Switch effects — Volt Switch, U-turn, Parting Shot, Baton Pass (partial — damage applies, switch doesn't)
  • OHKO moves — Horn Drill, Guillotine, Sheer Cold, Fissure (not)
  • Delayed damage — Future Sight, Doom Desire (not)
  • Invulnerability — chargingMove.invisible flag exists but accuracy bypass not yet checked
  • Specific move quirks — Body Slam paralysis (treated as stat drop proxy), Facade double power when statused, Wake-Up Slap double vs sleeping, etc.

docs.title: Adding a New Move
docs.text:
1. Find the move's MoveId entry (all 789 already listed)
  2. Add the declaration to the appropriate type family file
  3. Chain .attr() calls for secondary effects
  4. Set .implemented() status honestly
  5. Automatically registered at boot via registerAll() — no other changes needed

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The world map is a procedurally generated node graph in the style of Slay the Spire. The player navigates nodes to progress toward each gym leader. The map visual transforms to reflect the current grief stage zone.

docs.title: Combat Nodes
docs.text:
• Wild Encounter — standard Pokémon battle, can attempt to catch
  • Trainer Battle — NPC trainer, no catching permitted
  • Elite Trainer — harder trainer battle, better rewards
  • Gym Leader — fixed story node, major battle
  • E4 Member — fixed late game node, any of the four E4 members

docs.title: Recovery Nodes
docs.text:
• Rest — heal party
  • Settlement — shop, heal, optional side dialogue with NPCs

docs.title: Story Nodes
docs.text:
• Lore — optional world building. Diary entries from drowned cities, historical records, pre-flood documents
  • NPC Encounter — dialogue, relationship building with recurring characters
  • Evan Encounter — rival appears, variable tone depending on story progress

docs.title: Reward Nodes
docs.text:
• Item Find — loot node, items including potential Rare Candy or EXP Share
  • Pokémon Rescue — find a displaced Pokémon, choose to take it or leave it. Free, no battle.
  • Mystery — unknown until arrival, could be any non-combat node type

docs.title: Evolution Nodes
docs.text:
• Common — friendship and miscellaneous evolutions
  • Uncommon — item, trade, and Hisuian evolutions including regional form options
  • Rare — early/illegal evolution of level-based Pokémon

Evolution nodes only spawn if a party member can use them.

docs.title: Map Structure
docs.text:
• Procedurally generated each run using a seeded graph algorithm
  • Divided into layers between each gym
  • Each layer has 1-3 nodes
  • Connections between layers follow rules — no dead ends, enforced variety
  • Player sees the path ahead but not everything
  • Branching paths converge at gym leaders

docs.title: Visual Identity
docs.text: The map transforms visually to reflect the current grief stage zone. Each zone has a distinct color palette, atmosphere, and emotional tone. See spawn_system.md for zone details.

docs.title: E4 Map
docs.text: The E4 has its own separate node map, like each gym. E4 members are scattered within nodes — no defined order. Any two can appear in any run in any sequence before the interruption occurs.

docs.title: Philosophy
docs.text: Solwara is not a play-and-done game. Replayability comes from meaningful choices and branching narrative rather than procedural generation.

docs.title: Branching Structure: The River Model
docs.text:
The game uses a river model — branches split at key decision points, converge at major story beats, then split again. This creates the feeling of meaningful choice without exponential content requirements.

Most selections change minor aspects of the experience rather than routing the player into an entirely different story. Examples: - Starter choice may trigger a special item to become available - Dialogue choices affect NPC relationships - Key story decisions affect which gym leaders are sympathetic vs hostile

docs.title: The Role System
docs.text: The players role — chosen invisibly through starter selection — is the primary branching axis. Each role experiences the same world but through a fundamentally different emotional lens. See story/roles.md.

docs.title: Gym Structure
docs.text:
• 8 gyms representing story arcs, not just badge collection
  • Each gym leader embodies a different relationship with the flood
  • The Elite Four equivalent exists but is interrupted by story — it is not possible to win
  • This interruption is a narrative gut punch: you think you are at the finish line

docs.title: Unlock System
docs.text:
• Roles expose their full starter pools through gameplay
  • Beating runs unlocks new starters (see pokemon/starters.md)
  • The Defector role is unlocked by completing all three launch roles
  • Discovery and secrets reward attentive players across multiple playthroughs

docs.title: No Multiplayer
docs.text: Multiplayer features are excluded due to save integrity concerns (client-side saves are trivially editable). The game is designed as a rich single-player experience. This may be revisited in the future if a server-side validation solution is implemented.

docs.title: Philosophy
docs.text: All Pokémon are available in Solwara. Every type must be represented in at least two grief stage zones. No Pokémon is locked to a single zone — everything can appear anywhere, just with different weights.

docs.title: Zone Weight System
docs.text:
Each Pokémon has a spawn weight per grief zone. Higher weight = more common in that zone.

  • Primary zones — appears frequently, feels thematically at home
  • Secondary zones — appears occasionally, makes thematic sense
  • Tertiary zones — very rare, surprising to find here
  • Absent — does not appear (should be rare — most Pokémon have at least two zones)

Spawn weights are stored in the master Pokémon JSON file and can be tuned without touching code.

docs.title: Zone Thematic Flavor
docs.text:
Each grief zone has a visual identity and emotional tone that informs which Pokémon feel at home there:

Zone Tone Natural Pokémon Flavor Denial Aggressively normal, warm Normal types, familiar, non-threatening Pain & Guilt Muted, heavy, somber Ghost, Poison, lingering types Anger & Bargaining Red, stormy, jagged Fighting, Fire, aggressive types Depression & Loneliness Cold, vast, empty Ice, alone-natured, slow types Upward Turn Warming, sparking Electric, emerging types Reconstruction Ordered, geometric Rock, Steel, structured types Acceptance Bioluminescent, gorgeous Water, diverse, at peace Hope Open sky, light Flying, bright, soaring types

docs.title: Rescue Node Spawns
docs.text: See catch_system.md for full rescue node rules. Rescue nodes draw from a global pool weighted by BST — not zone specific.

docs.title: JSON Structure
docs.text: ```json "spawnData": { "nodeTypes": ["wild", "rescue"], "zoneWeights": { "denial": 2, "pain": 15, "anger": 3, "depression": 12, "upward": 2, "reconstruction": 1, "acceptance": 4, "hope": 2 } } ```

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Solwara is divided into 8 zones, each representing a stage of grief. Zones are traversed sequentially. Each zone has its own seeded procedural map, visual theme, spawn pool, and level cap tied to the zone's gym leader.

docs.title: Zone Order
docs.text:
Index Zone Key Zone Name Level Cap Gym Leader 0 denial Shock & Denial 20 Jon (Normal) 1 pain Pain & Guilt 30 Reese (Poison) 2 anger Anger & Bargaining 35 Mari (Fighting) 3 depression Depression & Loneliness 40 Lupa (Ice) 4 upward The Upward Turn 45 Bobby (Electric) 5 reconstruction Reconstruction 50 Bram (Steel/Rock) 6 acceptance Acceptance 55 Lena (Water) 7 hope Hope & New Meaning 60 Sol (Flying)

Post-game level caps: E4 = 80, Evan = 100.

docs.title: Visual Themes
docs.text:
Each zone has a distinct visual identity applied to the map, node icons, and battle backgrounds.

Zone Background Accent Mood denial Warm amber/brown Gold Familiar, almost normal — wrong if you look too closely pain Deep purple Violet glow Heavy, claustrophobic, suffocating anger Deep red/crimson Flame orange Chaotic, hot, aggressive depression Dark navy Steel blue Sparse, cold, vast emptiness upward Dark forest green Lime First hints of life returning reconstruction Charcoal grey Slate Industrial, purposeful, unfinished acceptance Deep teal Bioluminescent cyan Oceanic, still, beautiful hope Deep midnight blue Soft gold/white Open sky, expansive, forward-looking

docs.title: Node Spawn Weights
docs.text:
Each zone biases toward certain node types. Weights below are relative (higher = more common):

Type denial pain anger depression upward reconstruction acceptance hope wild 8 6 8 5 6 6 5 6 trainer 6 5 8 5 5 6 4 5 elite 2 2 4 2 3 3 2 3 rest 3 4 2 5 4 3 5 4 lore 3 4 2 5 3 3 4 3 npc 2 3 2 3 3 2 4 3 item 3 2 3 2 4 4 3 3 rescue 2 3 1 3 3 2 4 3 mystery 2 3 2 3 2 2 2 3 evolution 1 1 1 1 1 2 2 2

Rival, settlement, rest (floor 13), item (floor 8), wild (floor 0), and gym (floor 14) are placed by floor guarantee rules, not weights.

docs.title: Level Cap System
docs.text:
• Pokémon cannot gain EXP beyond the zone's level cap
  • Bench Pokémon receiving EXP Share also respect the cap
  • Rare Candy bypasses the current zone's cap (absolute ceiling: level 100)
  • Level is shown bold on the party screen; turns soft red (#d06060) when at cap
  • Battle HUD level text also turns red when at cap

docs.title: The Hope Badge
docs.text: Sol (Gym 8) is never completed in a normal run — the Unforgotten Party interrupts during Gym 8. The Hope Badge remains greyed out in the badge case throughout the climax, and is only earned by completing the game with all three launch roles (Hero, The Doctor, The Accepted).

docs.title: Implementation
docs.text:
// Zone order constant — single source of truth
export const ZONE_ORDER: ZoneKey[] = [
  'denial', 'pain', 'anger', 'depression',
  'upward', 'reconstruction', 'acceptance', 'hope',
];

// Level caps
export const ZONE_LEVEL_CAPS: Record<ZoneKey | 'e4', number> = {
  denial: 12, pain: 20, anger: 28, depression: 36,
  upward: 42, reconstruction: 48, acceptance: 54, hope: 60, e4: 80,
};

docs.title: System Overview
docs.text:
• Each role has a pool of 5 starters
  • The default starter is available from the beginning
  • Beating a run unlocks a random starter from that roles pool
  • Legendary starters have a 10% unlock chance; guaranteed if all others are unlocked
  • Unlockable starters may bend 1-2 mainline evolution rules for thematic fit
  • No fakemon. All starters are existing Pokémon.
  • The Fire/Water/Grass triangle is not required.

docs.title: The Hero Pool
docs.text:
Brave, selfless, perhaps reckless. Carries the weight of others expectations.

Starter Status Notes Machop Default Classic, humble, hardworking. Follows all mainline rules. Riolu Unlock Friendship evolution bend. Iconic Hero Pokémon. Rookidee Unlock Scrappy underdog who rises to Corviknight. Shinx Unlock Brave, protective, fights for others. Gible Legendary (10%) Pseudo-legendary ceiling. Tiny and comedic origin, fearsome destination.

docs.title: The Doctor Pool
docs.text:
Curious, driven, interventionist. Brilliant but arrogant. Always moving, always solving. Playing this role should feel like being The Doctor from Doctor Who.

Starter Status Notes Abra Default Genius, eccentric, nonviolent, underestimated. Teleports away from danger. Sinistea Unlock Ancient soul in an unlikely vessel. "I am older than I look." Solosis Unlock Challenge pick. Unconventional, slow, resilient. Non-violent by nature. Elgyem Unlock Mysterious origin, otherworldly, observant. Arrived from somewhere else. Dreepy Legendary (10%) Ancient being reborn, haunts a world it no longer recognizes. Carries enormous loss.

docs.title: The Accepted Pool
docs.text:
Calm, unbothered, at peace — but perhaps for the wrong reasons.

Starter Status Notes Mareanie Default Thrives in a hostile world without malice. Just exists. Shellos Unlock Adapts completely to its environment. Two forms, no resistance. Komala Unlock Permanently asleep. Unbothered by literally everything. Wishiwashi Unlock Looks helpless. Reveals something enormous when pushed. Lapras Legendary (10%) Gentle giant. In the flooded world, a symbol of quiet survival and ancient wisdom.

docs.title: The Defector Pool (Unlockable Role)
docs.text:
Unlocked by completing the game with all three launch roles. Starter pool to be designed after launch roles are fully written.

Starter Status Notes TBD — Pool TBD. Tinkaton is a candidate — rebellious, defiant, underdog energy.

docs.title: Setup
docs.text: The player has spent the entire game chasing Groudon rumors alongside — and sometimes against — The Unforgotten Party. The party believes they are close to a breakthrough. The player arrives first and finds not Groudon, but Kyogre.

docs.title: The Encounter
docs.text:
This is not a battle.

Kyogre does not attack. It has been waiting. It is the only living being that knows the truth, and it has carried that knowledge alone since The War while the world blamed it.

Kyogre shows the player a vision — the actual final moments of the conflict. Groudon falling. The waters rising. Kyogre, alone, surviving something it never asked to be part of.

docs.title: The Revelation
docs.text:
Groudon is dead. Has been since The War. The Unforgotten Party has been searching for something that no longer exists.

Kyogre is not a monster. It is the only living witness to the truth — and it has been alone with that truth while the world mourned through it.

docs.title: The Choice
docs.text:
The player must decide: - Tell The Unforgotten Party the truth — destroy their hope, force acceptance - Protect them from it — leave the delusion intact

This choice and its consequences are the emotional climax of the game. There is no clean answer.

docs.title: Why Kyogre
docs.text: Kyogre as truth-teller is the most poetic possible resolution. The thing the world fears, blames, and cannot forgive is the only one who can set them free. The player has to go to it — not to fight, not to capture — just to listen.

docs.title: Structure
docs.text: The E4 is not a linear gauntlet — it is a separate node map like each gym. E4 members are scattered within the nodes with no defined order. Any two can appear in any run in any sequence.

docs.title: The Interruption
docs.text:
• After the 2nd E4 fight, each subsequent node has an escalating chance of interruption:
  • Next node after 2nd fight: 50%
  • Following node: 75%
  • Node after that: 100%
  • The interruption is a mysterious emergency requiring the players specific attention
  • The caller is unknown at the time — it is Kyogre
  • This leads directly into the climax sequence

docs.title: Mark — Naval Commander
docs.text:
• Gender: Male
  • Party relationship: Opportunist — supports whoever is politically advantageous
  • Ace: Garry (Gyarados) with Supreme Overlord
  • Pool flavor: Disciplined, naval, built for sustained combat. Water, Steel, Fighting adjacent.
  • Frenemy dynamic: Most outwardly composed. Quiet, precise digs. Devastating without raising his voice.

docs.title: Sally — Small Town Mayor/Diplomat
docs.text:
• Gender: Non-conforming
  • Party relationship: Complicated neutral
  • Ace: Arceus with Libero
  • Pool flavor: No type restrictions. Approachable, resilient, dangerously underestimated.
  • Frenemy dynamic: The mediator. Knows where all the bodies are buried. Warm but never foolish.

docs.title: Tau — Scientist/Oceanographer
docs.text:
• Gender: Male
  • Party relationship: Opponent — deals in evidence, finds the partys mythology dangerous
  • Ace: Aerodactyl with Storm Drain
  • Pool flavor: Research oriented, ancient, analytical. Fossil Pokémon, things that survived against the odds.
  • Frenemy dynamic: Argues constantly but always factually. Exhausting to Mark. Sally quietly agrees with him more than they admit.

docs.title: Jabari — School Teacher
docs.text:
• Gender: Male
  • Party relationship: True believer — genuine, warm, deeply personal
  • Ace: Dialga with Quick Draw
  • Pool flavor: History, memory, preservation. Ancient Pokémon, things that carry the weight of time.
  • Frenemy dynamic: Most openly emotional. Tau and Jabari argue constantly. Sally loves him and worries about him.

docs.title: E4 Pool System
docs.text: Each member has a scripted ace plus 5 Pokémon drawn randomly from a pool of 25 per member. Fighting any E4 member twice never feels identical.

docs.title: Frenemy Dynamic
docs.text: The four E4 members are frenemies — people who have been in each others orbit so long they cannot tell if they actually like each other. Inspired by the relationship between Hiruluk and Kureha from One Piece. Genuine bond expressed almost entirely through antagonism and competitive one-upmanship.

docs.title: Level Range
docs.text: E4 battles: level 70-100. Hard cap for the player at this stage: 80.

docs.title: Final Boss — Evan
docs.text:
Evan went with the player to Kyogre. They witnessed the same truth — Groudon is dead, restoration is impossible. And they cannot accept it.

Evan fights because accepting the truth means accepting that their entire identity was built around an impossibility. They have spent their whole life defined by Archies name. The flood, the guilt, the generational shame. And now it was all for nothing.

  • Level: 100 (hard cap)
  • Starter: Flamigo with Scrappy (hard set)
  • Tone: This fight is to the death. Glazed over but hinted at. Dark enough to land, tasteful enough for the age range.

Evan is not a villain. They are someone who could not stop running in time.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Unforgotten Party is the primary antagonist faction of Solwara. They are a legitimate political party with genuine public support, not a traditional evil team lurking in caves.

docs.title: Slogan
docs.text:
"The Land Remembers."

A rallying cry that sounds noble — an act of defiance, a vow to never forget what was lost. By the end of the game the player understands the dark implication: the land does not remember. The land is gone. The people need to let it go.

docs.title: Nature
docs.text:
• Publicly: advocates for restoration research and reclamation efforts
  • Privately: actively pursuing the awakening of Groudon
  • They do genuine good — disaster relief, housing for survivors, infrastructure
  • Their extremism is revealed gradually as the player peels back layers

docs.title: Their Position on Kyogre
docs.text:
The Unforgotten Party correctly identifies Kyogre as a victim — weaponized by Team Aqua, hated unfairly by Team Magma. Pokémon are not at fault for The War. This is one of the few things they have right.

They want to awaken Groudon not out of hatred for Kyogre but to rebalance the world. They frame it as freeing Groudon rather than weaponizing it — consciously trying to avoid repeating Team Aquas mistake.

Except they are repeating it anyway. Just with different justifications.

docs.title: The Tragic Flaw
docs.text:
Groudon is dead. The Unforgotten Partys entire plan is built on an impossibility. Nobody knows this — not even the party leadership. They are acting in complete good faith toward an unreachable goal.

This is not a story about institutional corruption or conspiracy. It is a story about collective delusion — grief pointed in an impossible direction by sincere people who never learned the truth.

docs.title: Visual Identity
docs.text:
• Colors: Terracotta, ochre, deep amber (dry land, soil, rock) with clean white accents
  • Aesthetic: Clean, professional, almost corporate. Inspiring propaganda that looks genuinely hopeful.
  • Symbol: A mountain peak or sunrise emerging from water — noble on the surface, with Groudons markings subtly worked into the negative space
  • Architecture: Deliberately references pre-flood building styles

docs.title: Failure
docs.text: When their plan collapses it should not feel like defeating Team Rocket. It should feel like watching someone finally have to accept a truth they have been running from for years.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
8 gyms representing the 8 stages of grief. Each leader embodies a different relationship with the flood. Defeating a gym leader is not just a mechanical milestone — it is an emotional chapter.

Gyms follow a Slay the Spire style map structure. The player navigates nodes to reach the leader.

All gym leaders field 5 Pokémon, except Lena (6). No Mega Evolutions or Gigantamax forms permitted.

docs.title: Gym 1 — Shock & Denial
docs.text:
Leader: Jon | Type: Normal | Badge: The Denial Badge | Level Range: ~20

Personality: Warm, welcoming, genuinely kind. Has built an entire performance of pre-flood normalcy and convinced a community to perform it with him. Not sinister — heartbreaking.

The Gym: Looks like a bustling pre-flood city. Art depicting skyscrapers, volcanoes, vast plains. The closer you look the wronger it feels. Nobody acknowledges the ocean visible through the windows.

Team: | Pokémon | Notes | |---|---| | Lillipup | Innocent, familiar, safe | | Stufful | Looks harmless, hits harder than expected | | Cinccino | Quick, polished, put-together exterior | | Dudunsparce (Ace) | Changed but did not change. Denial in Pokémon form. |

Note: Dudunsparce is hard set — evolution mechanics in Solwara differ from mainline.

docs.title: Gym 2 — Pain & Guilt
docs.text:
Leader: Reese (Female) | Type: Poison | Badge: The Painful Badge | Level Range: ~25-30

Personality: Quiet, exhausted in a way that goes bone deep. Has spent her life cataloguing the names of everyone lost in the flood. Thought documenting the pain would process it. Instead she is drowning in it. Not unkind — just carrying too much.

The Gym: Every surface covered in names. Floor, ceiling, walls — layers of handwriting in different colors, different ages of ink. Others have come to add names. It became a pilgrimage site without her intending it. She cannot leave now — she is the keeper of everyone elses pain.

Team: | Pokémon | Notes | |---|---| | Mareanie | Absorbs toxicity quietly | | Haunter | Lingers, cannot move on | | Seviper | Carries venom it did not choose | | Drapion (Ace) | Resilient, scarred, still standing |

docs.title: Gym 3 — Anger & Bargaining
docs.text:
Leader: Mari (Female) | Type: Fighting | Badge: The Anger Badge | Level Range: ~30-35

Personality: Inherited fury. Lost something specific and concrete through family history. The Unforgotten Partys loudest public voice in her region — not a formal member, just someone whose rage aligns with their goals. Does not waste words.

The Gym: Active, chaotic training ground. Constant noise, people sparring, no stillness anywhere. Stillness means thinking. Thinking means feeling. Architecture partially destroyed and rebuilt repeatedly.

Team: | Pokémon | Notes | |---|---| | Primeape | Pure unfiltered rage | | Bewear | Aggressive affection — love expressed as force | | Passimian | Coordinated, team-focused anger | | Slaking/Fighting type (Ace) | Rage...bargain...rage...bargain. Truant as grief mechanic. |

Note: Slaking is hard set as Fighting type via type-changing node mechanic.

docs.title: Gym 4 — Depression & Loneliness
docs.text:
Leader: Lupa (Male) | Type: Ice | Badge: The Lonely Badge | Level Range: ~35-40

Personality: Did not dramatically collapse — just slowly stopped. Keeps routines, maintains the gym, does what is expected. Gentle with the player. Speaks like someone reading from a script of how humans are supposed to interact. Nobody home behind the eyes.

The Gym: Vast, sparse, cold. Huge windows over open ocean. Long corridors with almost no trainers. The silence is architectural — Lupa designed it that way without realizing.

Team: | Pokémon | Notes | |---|---| | Snorunt | Small, isolated, barely there | | Beartic | Solitary, drifting, cold endurance | | Cryogonal | No face. Just geometry. Alone by nature. | | Glaceon (Ace) | Chose isolation. Froze itself into something beautiful and untouchable. |

docs.title: Gym 5 — The Upward Turn
docs.text:
Leader: Bobby (Female) | Type: Electric | Badge: The Turning Badge | Level Range: ~40-45

Personality: Got better without a grand narrative. One morning something felt different and she watered her plants. Then fixed a broken window. Then started talking to her neighbors. Small choices accumulating into something resembling living. Slightly awkward about her own recovery — feels she has not earned the lightness.

The Gym: Bright, slightly chaotic, visibly unfinished. Plants everywhere, some thriving, some recovering. Mismatched furniture. Evidence of someone redecorating mid-thought. First gym where actual genuine laughter is heard.

Team: | Pokémon | Notes | |---|---| | Emolga | Sudden bursts of energy, unpredictable | | Pachirisu | Small joy, persistent | | Dedenne | Tiny, persistent, refuses to quit | | Jolteon | Sharp, reactive, newly awake | | Ampharos (Ace) | Steady warm light. A lighthouse. |

docs.title: Gym 6 — Reconstruction
docs.text:
Leader: Bram (Male) | Type: Steel/Rock | Badge: The Rebuilding Badge | Level Range: ~45-50

Personality: Grieved with his hands. The day after the worst moment of his life he picked up tools and started building. Has not really stopped since. His settlement is the most structurally impressive in Solwara. The question nobody asks — is he building because he believes in the future, or because stopping means feeling everything he has been outrunning?

The Gym: Active construction site. Literal scaffolding, materials everywhere, work happening around the player. Trainers are workers on break. The badge is earned in a room that was not finished when the player arrived.

Team: | Pokémon | Notes | |---|---| | Boldore | Raw material, unrefined, powerful | | Magneton | Precise, structural, holds things together | | Steelix | Built from something ordinary into something unbreakable | | Bastiodon | Immovable, protective, a wall against the past | | Aggron (Ace) | Builds its own armor. Reconstructs itself constantly. |

docs.title: Gym 7 — Acceptance
docs.text:
Leader: Lena (Female) | Type: Water | Badge: The Accepted Badge | Level Range: ~50-55

Personality: Lost as much as anyone. Will tell you plainly if you ask — no drama, no performance. Does not remember exactly when it shifted. One day the ocean looked beautiful instead of cruel. People come from all over Solwara just to sit with someone who seems okay. She makes tea. She listens. She does not try to fix anything.

The Gym: The most beautiful location in the game. Open water all around, structures built with the ocean rather than against it. Light filtering through shallow water overhead. Bioluminescent Pokémon visible in the depths below glass floors. Completely, achingly still.

Team: | Pokémon | Notes | |---|---| | Slowbro | Unbothered, deep, arrived somewhere long ago | | Alomomola | Caretaker, heals naturally, gives without asking | | Vaporeon | Dissolved into the water, became part of it | | Primarina | Sings for the lost, keeps singing anyway | | Milotic | Beauty found in turbulent origins | | Suicune (Ace) | Purifies what it touches. Moves forward always. |

Note: Suicune is a legendary — hard set at appropriate level.

docs.title: Gym 8 — Hope & New Meaning
docs.text:
Leader: Sol (Male) | Type: Flying | Badge: The Hope Badge | Level Range: ~55-60

Personality: Grew up looking up. While everyone around him looked at what the water took, he watched the sky. Birds still fly. Clouds still move. The sun still rises every morning without fail. Found meaning in the things the flood could not touch. Not naive about the loss — just chose to look at what is not underwater.

The Gym: The most open, airy, breathtaking location in the game. All sky and light and soaring Pokémon.

The Interruption: The player never completes this gym in a normal run. The Unforgotten Party makes their move during Gym 8. The Hope Badge remains grayed out in the badge case for the entire climax — a constant reminder of what was interrupted.

Unlocking Sol: Fighting Sol becomes possible after beating the game with all three launch roles (Hero, The Doctor, The Accepted). The Hope Badge is the ultimate completion reward. The last thing in the badge case to light up.

Team: | Pokémon | Notes | |---|---| | Swellow | Fast, free, never looks down | | Altaria | Soft, dreamy, floats above it all | | Skarmory | Built for endurance, survives anything | | Flygon | Drifts where the wind takes it | | Staraptor | Fierce hope — not passive, active | | Talonflame (Ace) | Flies highest, burns brightest, never stops climbing |

docs.title: Rival: Evan
docs.text:
• Gender: Non-conforming
  • Starter: Flamigo with Scrappy ability (hard set)
  • Background: Native Solwarian, distant cousin of Archie (Team Aqua leader). Knows about the family connection. Carries generational guilt for something they did not do.
  • Relationship: Never fully becomes the players friend but is not their enemy either. Disdain masks exhaustion. Respects the player eventually — grudgingly, unspoken.
  • Arc: Spent their whole life defined by something they did not do. Journey is learning to carry the family history without being crushed by it.

Note: This game takes place 100 years after Team Aqua. Archie is a historical figure to Evan, not a living relative.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The player chooses a role at the start of the game by selecting a starter. The role is not explained to the player — they simply pick a Pokémon. On replays, players recognize how their role shaped their entire experience.

Roles are displayed at starter selection with a short evocative description. The narrative meaning is left for the player to discover.

docs.title: The Hero
docs.text:
"Others look to you for answers."

Personality: Brave, selfless, perhaps reckless. Carries the weight of others expectations. Inspires those around them without always knowing how.

Arc: The Hero spends the game being looked to for a solution. The "there is no fixing this" revelation hits them hardest — they were supposed to be the one who saved everything.

docs.title: The Doctor
docs.text:
"You believe every problem has a solution."

Personality: Curious, driven, interventionist. Brilliant but sometimes arrogant. Always moving, always trying to fix things. Deeply empathetic but gets people hurt through conviction. Eccentric and unpredictable. Carries enormous loss.

Arc: Self-driven rather than externally driven. The Doctor runs toward danger believing they can solve it. Their acceptance arc is about learning that some things cannot be solved — only witnessed.

Inspiration: Deliberately evokes The Doctor from Doctor Who in player feel and emotional journey.

docs.title: The Accepted
docs.text:
"You have already made your peace."

Personality: Calm, still, unbothered. Has made peace with the flooded world — but perhaps too easily. There is a difference between resignation and genuine acceptance, and this role begins at the wrong one.

Arc: The most nuanced arc. The Accepted already knows the answer but has to learn why it is the right answer. Their journey is discovering that their peace was actually avoidance.

docs.title: The Defector (Unlockable)
docs.text:
Unlocked by completing the game with all three launch roles.

Personality: Conflicted. Formerly loyal to The Unforgotten Party. Carries guilt.

Arc: Begins aligned with the faction and switches sides. The player experiences the villains ideology from the inside before rejecting it. Reveals hidden lore and the full picture of the story.

Note: The Defector role is reserved for post-launch or late development. The other three roles must be fully written first as the Defector arc depends on their context.

docs.title: Unlock System
docs.text:
• Each role has a pool of 5 starters
  • Default starter is available immediately
  • Beating a run unlocks a random starter from that roles pool
  • Legendary starters have a 10% unlock chance
  • If only the legendary starter remains locked, next unlock is guaranteed (100%)
  • Beating a role 4 times guarantees the legendary unlock path

docs.title: Primary Theme: Acceptance
docs.text:
The central theme of Solwara is the difference between resignation and genuine acceptance.

The player spends the entire game believing they are working toward restoring the world to its prior state. The climax reframes everything: the world is not broken. It changed. And the real antagonist of the story is the collective inability to let go.

"There is no fixing this."

This is not a nihilistic message. The ending should feel bittersweet but ultimately warm. The world changed, people were lost, but look at what became of it — new life, new culture, new beauty. A eulogy that turns into a celebration.

docs.title: Secondary Theme: Collective Delusion
docs.text: No one in this story is purely a villain. The Unforgotten Party acts in good faith. Their plan is impossible not because they are evil but because the truth — that Groudon is dead — has never been discovered. Everyone is pointed in an impossible direction with sincere hearts.

docs.title: Age Range & Tone
docs.text:
• Target audience: Teens to adults
  • Awareness: Children will play it; content must remain accessible
  • Model: Pixar approach — surface is fun and accessible, underlying themes land harder for older players
  • Limits: Grief and loss are present but not graphic. Moral ambiguity without cynicism. Death implied, not shown.

docs.title: The Ending
docs.text:
The player discovers the truth through Kyogre itself. Not a battle — a vision. Kyogre shows what actually happened to Groudon. The player must then decide whether to tell The Unforgotten Party the truth and destroy their hope, or protect them from it.

The ending should feel earned. Acceptance is not defeat.

docs.title: Canon Basis
docs.text: Solwara is set in a world where the events of Pokémon Sapphire were not stopped. Archie succeeded in awakening Kyogre, the rain never ceased, and sea levels rose catastrophically over the following generations.

docs.title: The Flood
docs.text:
The flooding was not instantaneous — it happened over decades. The grandparents of todays inhabitants remember dry land. There are photographs, oral histories, and ruins of the old world still visible beneath the waves.

The region most affected is Oceania — Australia, New Zealand, Papua New Guinea, Indonesia — now a vast ocean with scattered land patches. The largest usable land masses are approximately 250 miles across, representing the peaks of mountain ranges, volcanic highlands, and elevated terrain that survived the inundation.

docs.title: Kyogre
docs.text: Kyogre is not a villain. The world has a complicated relationship with it: - The Unforgotten Party — correctly identifies Kyogre as a victim, weaponized by Team Aqua and hated unfairly by Team Magma. Pokémon are not at fault. - General population — mixed feelings, some instinctive blame - The truth — Kyogre is the only living witness to what actually happened to Groudon

docs.title: Groudon
docs.text:
Groudon is dead. It died during the conflict with Kyogre — the event the world calls "The War." Nobody knows this. The Unforgotten Party searches for Groudon believing restoration is possible. Their hope is built on a tragedy no one has discovered yet.

Whether the truth was covered up or simply lost is unknown — but the answer is that nobody knows. Everyone is acting in good faith toward an impossible goal.

docs.title: The World Now
docs.text: Two to three generations have passed since The War. A new culture has formed: - Underwater cities built into drowned ruins - Surface settlements on land patches - A maritime economy connecting the scattered islands - Deep political division between those who have accepted the new world and those who want the old one back
